LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFFS CRYSTAL LEI AND BRYANT FU'S MOTION FOR ORDER SETTING ATTORNEYS FEES IN FAVOR OF PLAINTIFFS AS PREVAILING PARTIES ON DEMAS YANS 2016 MOTION TO STRIKE IS GRANTED. NO OPPOSITION FILED. MS. LEI AND MR. FU ARE AWARDED ATTORNEY'S FEES IN THE AMOUNT OF $53,910. THE APPEAL OF THE APRIL 28, 2016 ORDER DENYING THE ANTI-SLAPP MOTION DOES NOT STAY THE ENTIRE CASE. IN VARIAN MEDICAL SYSTEMS, INC. V. DELFINO (2005) 35 CAL.4TH 180, WHICH DEFENDANTS CITE IN THEIR "NOTICE OF STAY OF PROCEEDINGS," THE COURT HELD THAT THE TRIAL COURT LACKED JURISDICTION TO RESOLVE THE MERITS OF THE CASE AFTER A PARTY APPEALED AN ORDER DENYING AN ANTI-SLAPP MOTION TO STRIKE. ATTORNEY'S FEES, HOWEVER, IS A COLLATERAL MATTER THAT DOES NOT AFFECT THE APPEAL WITHIN THE MEANING OF CCP 916. (ROBERTSON V. RODRIGUEZ (1995) 36 CAL.APP.4TH 347, 360 (TRIAL COURT HAS JURISDICTION TO ISSUE FEE AWARD DESPITE APPEAL OF ORDER GRANTING ANTI-SLAPP MOTION).) ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN. CLERK: ROSIE NOGUERA. NOT REPORTED.

LAW AND MOTION 302, THE COURT TO ADDRESS THE ISSUE OF WHETHER THE MAY 12, 2017 ORDER WAS ERRONEOUS AND SHOULD BE VACATED IS BEING CONTINUED TO JULY 26, 2017 ON THE COURT'S OWN MOTION. PLAINTIFFS CRYSTAL LEI AND BRYANT FU'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE THE ORDER TO VACATE PREFILING ORDER, AND REMOVE FROM JUDICIAL COUNCIL VEXATIOUS LITIGANT LIST PURSUANT TO CCP 663 IS DENIED. PLAINTIFFS ERRONEOUSLY SEEK TO USE CCP 663 AS THE PROCEDURAL VEHICLE FOR THEIR MOTION. THE PLAIN LANGUAGE OF THAT STATUE SHOWS THAT IT ONLY PERTAINS TO A JUDGMENT AND ONLY AUTHORIZES A COURT TO ENTER A NEW JUDGMENT WHEN THERE HAS BEEN A LEGAL ERROR. A MOTION UNDER CCP 663 DOES NOT LIE TO VACATE A COURT ORDER OTHER THAN A JUDGMENT. (PAYNE V. RADER (2008) 167 CAL.APP.4TH 1569, 1575 (OVERRULED ON OTHER GROUNDS IN RYAN V. ROSENFELD ( CAL. SUP. CT. JUNE 15, 2017) 2017 WL 2589515); FORMAN V. KNAPP PRESS (1985) 173 CAL.APP.3D 200.) HOWEVER, THE COURT HAS DECIDED ON ITS OWN MOTION AND, PER THE AUTHORITY OF LE FRANCOIS V. GOEL (2005) 35 CAL. 4TH 1094, TO ADDRESS THE ISSUE OF WHETHER THE MAY 12, 2017 ORDER WAS ERRONEOUS AND SHOULD BE VACATED. THE COURT WILL TREAT PLAINTIFFS' MOVING PAPERS AS THE MOVING PAPERS ON ITS OWN MOTION AND SET A HEARING ON ITS OWN MOTION ON JULY 26, 2017. DEFENDANT DEMAS YAN MAY FILE AND SERVE OPPOSITION PAPERS NO LATER THAN JULY 12, 2017 AND PLAINTIFFS MAY FILE AND SERVE RESPONSIVE PAPERS NO LATER THAN JULY 19, 2017. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN; CLERK: CYNTHIA HERBERT; NOT REPORTED.

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order To Vacate Prefiling Order And Remove From Judicial Council Vexatious Litigant List
Off Calendar Jan-27-2023 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Plaintiff BRYANT FU (Tel: 415-221-0969) in pro per;
Defendant DEMAS YAN (Tel: 415-867-5797) in pro per.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT DEMAS YAN's Motion For Order To Vacate Prefiling Order And Remove From Judicial Council Vexatious Litigant List.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Demas Yan's Motion For Order To Vacate Prefiling Order And Remove From Judicial Council Vexatious Litigant List is DENIED.

Yan has failed to show a material change in the facts upon which the order was granted and that the ends of justice would be served by vacating the order. (See Code Civ. Proc., 391.8, subd. (c).) Assuming without finding that the relief requested by Yan may be available by way of Code of Civil Procedure section 533, Yan has failed to show a material change in the facts upon which the vexatious litigant order was granted, that the law upon which the vexatious litigant order was granted has changed in any material way that cuts against the viability of the order, or that the ends of justice would be served by the modification or dissolution of the vexatious litigant order. (See Code Civ. Proc., 533.)

The judge who entered the order at issue is unavailable. The presiding judge of this court, accordingly, has assigned this motion to Hon. Joseph M. Quinn. (See Code Civ. Proc., 391.8, subd. (a).)

Order signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

Mtn To Set Aside The Order To Vacate Prefilinf Order
Off Calendar Jul-26-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Plaintiffs Crystal Lei (415.221.0969) and Bryant Fu (415.867.5322) appears as self-represented plaintiffs/moving parties

Attorney In Pro Per, Demas W. Yan of the law offices of Demas W. Yan (415.867.5797) appears as self-represented counsel for defendant/opposing party

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court confirms the tentative ruling with modification.

PLAINTIFFS CRYSTAL LEI and BRYANT FU'S Motion To Set Aside The Order To Vacate Prefiling Order is GRANTED. On its own motion, the court sets aside the May 12, 2017 order that vacated the April 13, 2015 order designating defendant Demas Yan a vexatious litigant and reinstates the April 13, 2015 order. The general rule that one superior court judge may not overrule another superior court is inapplicable when the first judge is unavailable. (Weil and Brown book at 9:324.4). Because of the CCP 170.6 challenge to him, Judge Ulmer is unavailable. The May 12, 2017 order was erroneously made. Mr. Yan meets the criteria for a vexatious litigant. A plaintiff, such as plaintiff Crystal Lei in this case, is authorized to file a motion declaring a defendant, such as Mr. Yan in this case, a vexatious litigant per CCP 391.7(a) because "any party" can file such a motion. By the May 12, 2017 order, Judge Ulmer vacated his April 13, 2015 order based on John. He determined that the motion to declare Mr. Yan a vexatious litigant was improper because Mr. Yan was a defendant in this case. (RT 5:17-22). Upon reviewing the record, however, the court believes that the May 12, 2017 order was erroneous. Plaintiffs have identified five timely litigations that Mr. Yan "commenced, prosecuted, or maintained" within the preceding seven-year period that were determined against him. (CCP 391(b)(1).) Those litigations are the following: CGC-07-465253, CGC-07-465863, CGC-08-478815, CGC-10-497990, and CGC-10-501321. While there is no basis to compel Mr. Yan to post security in this case, there is a basis to prevent him from filing new litigation without leave from the presiding judge under CCP 391.7. A requirement to post a security is distinct from a declaration that a litigant is a vexatious litigant. As a result of his being declared a vexatious litigant, Mr. Yan needs to obtain leave from the prefiling order to institute subsequent or new litigation, although he not need obtain leave to file an appeal in this action or any other case where is he is a defendant. Written order is signed and filed in open court. This written order is stayed for 30 days to allow Mr. Yan to seek writ review to the Court of Appeal. If a writ is filed and the Court of Appeal has not ruled on it by 8/28/17, the stay will remain until the Court of Appeal's ruling on the writ.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR No. 8189, email: ckaren@sftc.org. (D302)

RULING - THE COURT TO ADDRESS THE ISSUE OF WHETHER THE MAY 12, 2017 ORDER WAS ERRONEOUS AND SHOULD BE VACATED IS BEING CONTINUED TO JULY 26, 2017 ON THE COURT'S OWN MOTION. PLAINTIFFS CRYSTAL LEI and BRYANT FU'S Motion To Set Aside The Order To Vacate Prefiling Order, and remove from judicial council vexatious litigant list pursuant to CCP 663
is DENIED. Plaintiffs erroneously seek to use CCP 663 as the procedural vehicle for their motion. The plain language of that statue shows that it only pertains to a judgment and only authorizes a court to enter a new judgment when there has been a legal error. A motion under CCP 663 does not lie to vacate a court order other than a judgment. (Payne v. Rader (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 1569, 1575 (overruled on other grounds in Ryan v. Rosenfeld ( Cal. Sup. Ct. June 15, 2017) 2017 WL 2589515); Forman v. Knapp Press (1985) 173 Cal.App.3d 200.) However, the court has decided on its own motion and, per the authority of Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal. 4th 1094, to address the issue of whether the May 12, 2017 order was erroneous and should be vacated. The court will treat plaintiffs' moving papers as the moving papers on its own motion and set a hearing on its own motion on July 26, 2017. Defendant Demas Yan may file and serve opposition papers no later than July 12, 2017 and plaintiffs may file and serve responsive papers no later than July 19, 2017. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Not reported. =302/hk

MOTION And Notice Of Motion To Vacate The Final Arbitration Award Per Ccp 1286.2 And To Set Aside The Order Granting Allen Matkins Motion To Compel Arbitration Per Ccp 663
Off Calendar Jun-08-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - CRYSTAL LEI AND BRYANT FU, TEL. NO. 1-415-221-0969, PLAINTIFFS APPEARING IN PRO PER;
ATTY. EMILY CHARLEY OF HANSON BRIDGETT, TEL. NO. 1-415-995-5009 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANTS.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. PLAINTIFFS CRYSTAL LEI and BRYANT FU'S Motion To Vacate The Final Arbitration Award Per Ccp 1286.2 And To Set Aside The Order Granting Allen Matkins Motion To Compel Arbitration Per Ccp 663 is DENIED in its entirety. There is no basis to vacate the April 13, 2015 order that compelled arbitration. Plaintiffs erroneously cite CCP 663, which allows a court to vacate a judgment and enter a new judgment when there is an error at law. A motion under CCP 663 does not apply to an order compelling arbitration or a similar order. (Payne v. Rader (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 1569, 1575). The request to set aside the order compelling arbitration is, in effect, a motion for reconsideration that does not meet the requirements of CCP 1008. Plaintiffs previously filed a motion for reconsideration and sought appellate review of the April 13, 2015 order and did not prevail. Plaintiffs fail to establish a basis to vacate the arbitration award under the limited grounds in CCP 1286.2. Plaintiffs fail to show that Allen Matkins advance of the arbitration fees constituted a bribe or that the award should be vacated because of corruption or other undue means. JAMS Rule 6(c) specifically allows for advancing fees. The arbitrator did not ignore the parties' 4-hour agreement regarding the arbitration proceedings. The arbitrator correctly ruled that the fees would be split equally. Exhibit Q to the Fu declaration, which was copied to plaintiffs, does not show that Allen Matkins had improper communications with the arbitrator. Plaintiffs fail to demonstrate that there was a basis to recuse the arbitrator per CCP 1281.91. The arbitrator's asserted refusal to hear evidence and her decision not to continue the hearing are not grounds to vacate the award. Plaintiffs fail to establish their assertion that the arbitrator refused to hear material evidence. Plaintiffs' request for a continuance made a month before the hearing to locate an expert lacked good cause. Plaintiffs fail to show that the refusal to allow a personal videotaping device is grounds to vacate the award. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order and to comply with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR #8189 =302/hk

Demas Yans Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint As To: 1) First Cause Of Action Malicious Prosecution (Pursuant To Ccp 425.16) 2) Portions Of Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint(Pursuant To Ccp 435-436)
Off Calendar Apr-08-2016 Denied
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT DEMAS YAN'S ANTI-SLAPP (CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE 425.16) MOTION TO STRIKE AND CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE 435 MOTION TO STRIKE PORTIONS OF THE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT, AS TO: 1) FIRST CAUSE OF ACTION MALICIOUS PROSECUTION (PURSUANT TO CCP 425.16) 2) PORTIONS OF PLAINTIFFS FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT(PURSUANT TO CCP 435-436)

Appearances: William H.G. Norman of Cooper White & Cooper LLP (415.433.1900) appearing for plaintiffs Crystal Lei and Bryant Fu; Mark Lapham (925.837.9007) appearing for defendant Dennis Yan, specially appearing for defendant Cheuk Yan

Argued and the Court modifies its ruling as follows: Denied in their entirety. The malicious prosecution cause of action implicates section 425.16, but the fraud claim does not. Plaintiff demonstrates a probability of prevailing on the malicious prosecution cause of action. Defendant filed a cross-complaint in case no CGC-10-501321. That cross-complaint attempted to re-litigate the Chenery Project issues. The bankruptcy court rulings show that the cross-complaint lacked merit because the claims had already been resolved and only the trustee-not defendant-could assert them. On March 18, 2014, the Ninth Circuit affirmed the bankruptcy rulings and found defendant's appeal to be frivolous. Plaintiff timely filed the instant action on September 26, 2014. The cross-complaint favorably terminated in plaintiff's favor, the bankruptcy court rulings show that there was no probable cause to bring the cross-complaint, and defendant's vexatious litigation shows that he was maintaining the cross-complaint for an improper purpose, which demonstrates malice. (Sierra Club Foundation v. Graham (1999) 72 Cal.App.4th 1135, 1157.) The section 435 motion to strike is denied because none of the allegations sought to be stricken are clearly irrelevant or prejudicial. While plaintiff makes reference to lawsuits that in and of themselves cannot be the basis for a malicious prosecution claim, those lawsuits may be relevant to show malice or lack of probable cause. The Court finds the portion of the motion under the Anti-SLAPP statute is frivolous, entitling counsel to attorney fees in opposing that portion of the motion. Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order. Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR 5976
